---
title: "3. Seeing in four dimensions"
description: >
  Why 4D tic-tac-toe is hard, how the slice and cube views reveal hidden lines,
  and how to use the move-rating overlay and win probability to find winning and
  blocking moves.
output:
  rmarkdown::html_vignette:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  fig.width = 9, fig.height = 3,
  out.width = "100%",
  dpi = 120
)
```

```{r setup}
library(tesseractR)
```

> **Tutorial page 3 of 4.**
> Previous: [« Playing in the app](play-02-app.html) ·
> Next: [Playing from the R console »](play-04-api.html)

## Why four dimensions is hard

In the 3×3 game there are only 8 winning lines and you can see all of them at
once. On the tesseract there are **520**, and most of them are invisible in the
*Full* view because they jump between little boards. The whole skill of the game
is learning to *see* those hidden lines — and the tools below exist to help.

## Slicing the hypercube

A line that changes `k` or `l` looks scattered across the full board but becomes a
plain straight line once you fix the right axis. Consider a line that runs *down
the column of boards* — same `(i, j)`, with `l` going `0 → 3`:

```{r make-line}
b <- tsr_new_board()
moves <- list(
  c(1, 1, 0, 0), c(0, 0, 0, 0),   # X, O
  c(1, 1, 0, 1), c(0, 0, 0, 1),   # X, O
  c(1, 1, 0, 2), c(0, 0, 0, 2),   # X, O
  c(1, 1, 0, 3)                    # X completes l = 0..3 at i=1, j=1, k=0
)
for (m in moves) b <- tsr_move(b, m[1], m[2], m[3], m[4])
tsr_status(b)$winner   # 1 = X has won
```

In the **Full** view those four X's land in four *different* little boards, so the
win is easy to miss:

```{r full, fig.width = 7, fig.height = 7, fig.alt = "Full board: the winning line is spread across four different little boards."}
tsr_plot(b)
```

Fix the `k` axis at `0` and the same four cells line up vertically inside one
slice — now it reads as the straight line it really is:

```{r slice, fig.alt = "Slice view with k fixed: the winning line is a straight vertical line."}
tsr_plot_slice(b, axis = "k", at = 0)
```

The app exposes exactly this with the **Slice (2D)** and rotatable **Cube (3D)**
views — try every fixed axis when you are hunting for a threat:

```{r cube, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "The app's 3D cube view of a slice."}
knitr::include_graphics("screenshots/app-cube.png")
```

## Let the engine point at the good moves

You do not have to find every threat by eye. `tsr_rate_moves()` scores every
legal move for the player to move and flags the important ones. Suppose X already
has three in a line and it is X's turn:

```{r threat}
b <- tsr_new_board()
moves <- list(
  c(0, 0, 0, 0), c(3, 3, 3, 3),   # X, O
  c(1, 0, 0, 0), c(3, 3, 3, 2),   # X, O
  c(2, 0, 0, 0), c(3, 3, 3, 1)    # X, O  -> X has i = 0,1,2 at j=k=l=0
)
for (m in moves) b <- tsr_move(b, m[1], m[2], m[3], m[4])

rated <- tsr_rate_moves(b, method = "heuristic")
# the winning completion is flagged:
rated[rated$is_winning, c("cell", "i", "j", "k", "l", "win_prob", "is_winning")]
```

`tsr_rate_moves()` returns one row per legal move with its `win_prob`, a `rank`,
and the logical flags `is_best`, `is_winning`, and `is_blocking`. The same data
drives the **move-rating overlay** in the app — darker cells are higher
`win_prob`:

```{r overlay, echo = FALSE, fig.width = 7, fig.height = 7, fig.alt = "The app board with the purple move-rating overlay shading the stronger cells."}
knitr::include_graphics("screenshots/app-midgame.png")
```

### Blocking

The same flags catch *defence*. When your opponent is one move from a line,
the move that denies it is flagged `is_blocking`. A simple habit wins a lot of
games: **each turn, if any move is `is_winning`, take it; otherwise if any move is
`is_blocking`, play it.**

```{r block}
top <- rated[order(-rated$win_prob), c("cell", "win_prob", "is_best", "is_winning", "is_blocking")]
head(top, 3)
```

## A few rules of thumb

* **Central cells touch more lines.** Coordinates near the middle (`1` and `2`)
  sit on far more of the 520 lines than the corners — they are worth more.
* **Count threats, not marks.** A cell that advances two different lines at once
  is a *fork*: even a perfect defender can only block one.
* **Slice before you commit.** If a position feels murky, cycle the fixed axis
  through `i, j, k, l` — a threat that is invisible in one slice is usually
  obvious in another.
* **Watch the win-probability swing.** A move that drops your number sharply was
  probably a mistake; the Analysis tab shows exactly where those swings happened.

> Next: [Playing from the R console »](play-04-api.html)
